ElementDescription: The NDAR Global Unique Identifier (GUID) for research subject
Subject ID how it's defined in lab/project
Date on which the interview/genetic test/sampling/imaging/biospecimen was completed. MM/DD/YYYY
Age in months at the time of the interview/test/sampling/imaging.
Notes: Age is rounded to chronological month. If the research participant is 15-days-old at time of interview, the appropriate value would be 0 months. If the participant is 16-days-old, the value would be 1 month.

ElementDescription: Sex of subject at birth
Notes: M = Male; F = Female; O=Other; NR = Not reported

ElementDescription: Spoken language
Sign language
Picture, book, or board communication system (e.g., PECS, PODS)
An electronic communication device (e.g., iPad, Dynavox, Tobii)
When your child communicates, how many words/signs/pictures does your child put together or combine to create a message?
Indicates ''yes'' or ''no''
Uses names of objects
Requests or asks for things
Makes comments (e.g., ''Look! Dog!'')
Tells others what to do
Asks ''why'' questions
Tells you about an event that happened in the past
Has conversations (e.g., able to maintain back and forth conversation, does not cut others off)
Pronounces words correctly
Understands when told ''Yes'' or ''No''
Understands one-step directions (e.g., ''Go get your shoes'')
Responds when name is called
Understands two-step directions (e.g., ''Put the toy away, then go get your shoes.'')
Understands if-then directions (e.g., ''If you eat the broccoli, then you can have a cookie.'')
Is able to point to indicate what he or she wants
Points to share interest when not requesting (e.g., shows you an airplane in the sky)
Gestures (e.g., nods for yes, waves goodbye)
Makes appropriate eye context (e.g., looks at you when making a request but does not look too long or make people uncomfortable)
Uses facial expressions to show how he or she is feeling (e.g., smiles when happy, frowns when sad)
Combines eye contact, gestures, and facial expressions appropriately when speaking (e.g., sharing information using both speech and body language to communicate with the listener)
Appropriately gets someone's attention to start or end an interaction (e.g., calls name, taps shoulder, or makes eye contact before speaking
Understands personal space
Seems interested in interacting with children he/she knows
Responds appropriately to greetings from children he/she knows
Plays with a classmate (or other familiar child) with help from an adult
Plays with a classmate (or other familiar child) without help from an adult
Plays in a group of familiar children without help from an adult
Imitates or copies others as a means of learning
Plays simple social games (e.g., peek-a-boo, tag, hide-and-seek)
Understands social relationships (e.g., friendships, marriage)
Recognizes the emotions of other people
Identifies his or her own feelings
Understands that others may have a different point of view from his or her own
Shows remorse (being sorry) for mistakes
Handles criticism well
Offers comfort to others
